title: Common Annotation Formats
concept: Annotation formats like COCO JSON, Pascal VOC XML, and YOLO TXT store labeling coordinates and categories.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes drawing polygon masks more expensive than bounding boxes?

options.text: They require tracing exact pixel-level contours, requiring many more clicks and time
They must be saved in binary format instead of text
They require expensive high-resolution cameras to capture

exercises.explain: Polygon masks need many keypoints to outline shapes, making them time-intensive for human labelers.

cards.title: Translating clicks to code
cards.body: When an annotator draws a box, the computer must save it in a file. But how? Does it save the coordinates as pixels? Percentages? XML? JSON? In this lesson, we study the three dominant formats used to feed labels into vision architectures.

cards.title: Annotation Format
cards.body: An Annotation Format is a standardized schema for storing label metadata. The three main formats are: COCO JSON (hierarchical dictionary), Pascal VOC XML (structured markup), and YOLO TXT (normalized line-by-line values).

cards.title: Coordinates on a Map
cards.body: Imagine describing a location. You could use GPS coordinates (YOLO style), a address structure (VOC XML), or an index of landmarks (COCO JSON). Different systems prefer different formats, but they all point to the same spot.

cards.title: YOLO vs. VOC vs. COCO
cards.body: In YOLO txt, a line is: [class_id, x_center, y_center, width, height] normalized between 0 and 1. VOC uses raw pixels in XML: <xmin>, <ymin>. COCO uses a central JSON list linking image IDs to annotations.

cards.title: No single universal standard
cards.body: You might expect one universal format for all computer vision. In reality, you will frequently write conversion scripts because different model repositories expect different inputs (e.g., YOLO vs. PyTorch standard).

cards.title: Comparing Annotation Formats
cards.body: Three ways of representing the same bounding box:

left.text:
One .txt file per image.
Normalized coordinates (0 to 1).
Extremely compact.

right.text:
Single large .json file.
Hierarchical list structure.
Great for multi-task datasets.

exercises.prompt: Which file format is used by the COCO dataset to store annotations?
exercises.explain: COCO annotations are stored in a single large, structured JSON file containing images, annotations, and categories.

exercises.prompt: True or False: YOLO annotation coordinates are normalized between 0 and 1 relative to the image size.
exercises.explain: True. YOLO format normalizes the bounding box center, width, and height to decimals between 0 and 1.
Pascal VOC uses XML markup (Extensible Markup Language) to store bounding box bounds and classes.
exercises.sentence: Pascal VOC annotations are stored in structured ___ files, which use nested markup tags.

exercises.prompt: What does a line in a YOLO annotation file (e.g., '0 0.5 0.5 0.2 0.2') represent?
exercises.explain: Each line in a YOLO text file represents a single object's class ID, followed by normalized center coordinates and sizes.

options.text: Class ID, normalized X/Y center, and normalized width/height
Raw pixel coordinates of the bounding box corners
The RGB values of the center pixel of the image

exercises.prompt: You are converting a dataset from Pascal VOC (XML) to YOLO (TXT) format. The original XML box coordinates are in raw pixels. What mathematical step is required?

options.text: Divide the pixel coordinates by the image width and height to normalize the values between 0 and 1, and convert corner values to center coordinates.
Multiply the coordinates by the learning rate of the model.
Convert the values to binary strings using a base64 encoder.
Round the numbers to the nearest multiple of 16 to save space.

exercises.explain: YOLO expects center coordinates normalized by image size, while VOC uses raw pixel values of box edges (xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax).
